agent_output: {"executive_summary":"A 9x increase in P99 latency (200ms → 1800ms) over two weeks, with no deployment correlation, normal DB metrics, and no errors, strongly points to degradation in one or more of the three external API dependencies — most likely the geocoding service or carrier rates API experiencing gradual throughput throttling or increased response times. Secondary hypotheses include connection pool exhaustion, DNS resolution degradation, or subtle resource leaks (memory/thread/connection). The gradual onset without errors is the key diagnostic signal: it rules out code regressions and suggests environmental or upstream drift.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Logistics API Service","responsibility":"Orchestrates shipment quotes, tracking, and payment by composing calls to external services","boundaries":"Receives HTTP requests, coordinates external calls, returns aggregated responses"},{"name":"Geocoding Service (External)","responsibility":"Resolves addresses to coordinates for distance/zone calculations","boundaries":"Third-party API, latency and rate limits outside team control"},{"name":"Carrier Rates API (External)","responsibility":"Provides shipping rate quotes from carriers","boundaries":"Third-party API, may have variable response times based on carrier load"},{"name":"Payment Gateway (External)","responsibility":"Processes payment authorization and capture","boundaries":"Third-party API, typically well-provisioned but subject to retry/timeout behavior"},{"name":"Database","responsibility":"Stores shipment records, customer data, configuration","boundaries":"Internal, confirmed normal query times"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Geocoding Service","contract":"HTTP REST — address in, coordinates out","coupling_type":"temporal (synchronous)"},{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Carrier Rates API","contract":"HTTP REST — origin/dest/weight in, rates out","coupling_type":"temporal (synchronous)"},{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Payment Gateway","contract":"HTTP REST — charge request in, auth result out","coupling_type":"temporal (synchronous)"},{"from":"Logistics API","to":"Database","contract":"SQL/ORM queries","coupling_type":"temporal (synchronous)"}],"data_flow":"Request arrives → DB lookup for context → Geocoding call → Carrier Rates call (possibly sequential after geocoding) → Payment call (on checkout flows) → Response assembled and returned. If any external call is sequential and degraded, total latency compounds.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Sequential External Service Composition","rationale":"Likely current pattern — each external call blocks before the next, meaning latency is additive across all three services"},{"pattern":"Synchronous HTTP Integration","rationale":"Standard for request-scoped API orchestration, but creates temporal coupling that amplifies upstream latency"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Shipment Orchestration","classification":"core","aggregates":["Shipment","Quote","Route"]},{"name":"Payment Processing","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction"]},{"name":"Address Resolution","classification":"generic","aggregates":[]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Geocoding Service","downstream":"Shipment Orchestration","relationship":"Conformist — team has no control over geocoding API contract or performance"},{"upstream":"Carrier Rates API","downstream":"Shipment Orchestration","relationship":"Conformist — dependent on carrier's response times and data format"},{"upstream":"Payment Gateway","downstream":"Payment Processing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — typically has SLAs but team is downstream"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Investigate external API latency before internal causes","gains":"Highest probability root cause given the symptom profile (gradual, no errors, DB normal)","sacrifices":"Could miss a subtle internal issue like connection pool exhaustion","alternatives_considered":["Focus on internal profiling first","Blame database despite normal dashboard","Assume network infrastructure issue"]},{"decision":"Recommend adding per-dependency latency instrumentation","gains":"Definitive root cause identification, ongoing visibility","sacrifices":"Requires instrumentation work before the fix itself","alternatives_considered":["Guess and apply timeouts blindly","Add blanket caching without understanding which service is slow"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"External service call pattern","evolution_path":"Move from sequential to parallel calls where dependencies allow (e.g., geocoding and payment are independent). Introduce circuit breakers and timeouts."},{"area":"Caching layer for geocoding","evolution_path":"Geocoding results are highly cacheable — same address always maps to same coordinates. A cache here could eliminate the dependency entirely for repeated addresses."},{"area":"Carrier rates caching","evolution_path":"Short-TTL cache (30-60s) for identical route+weight queries could absorb burst traffic and reduce upstream dependency."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous external API dependency chain","reason":"All three external services are called synchronously in the request path. Any upstream degradation directly impacts user-facing latency with no isolation."},{"area":"Lack of per-service observability","reason":"The team sees aggregate latency and DB latency but apparently cannot isolate which external service is slow — this is a critical observability gap."}],"risks":[{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 1 (HIGH CONFIDENCE): One or more external APIs have gradually degraded. The geocoding or carrier rates service is the most likely culprit — geocoding services commonly introduce rate limiting or throttling that manifests as gradual latency increases rather than errors. At 10K req/day, the service may have crossed a soft throttling threshold.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediately add per-external-call latency logging (start/end timestamps for each outbound HTTP call). Compare P99 for each dependency independently. Check if the geocoding or rates provider has changed rate limit tiers or had published incidents in the past 2 weeks."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 2 (MEDIUM CONFIDENCE): Connection pool or HTTP client resource exhaustion. If the HTTP client reuses connections and a pool is slowly filling due to slow-draining responses from an upstream, new requests queue behind stale connections. This would cause gradual degradation even if individual queries eventually succeed.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Check HTTP client connection pool metrics — active connections, wait times, pool size. Look for increasing 'time to acquire connection' in logs. Verify keep-alive and idle timeout settings on the HTTP client."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 3 (MEDIUM CONFIDENCE): DNS resolution latency. If the service resolves external API hostnames on every request and the DNS resolver has degraded (cache eviction, upstream DNS issues), each external call adds DNS overhead. With 3 external calls per request, even 500ms DNS latency adds 1.5s.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Check DNS resolution times from the service host. Enable DNS caching in the HTTP client or at the OS level. Use tcpdump or dig to measure resolution times for the three external hostnames."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 4 (LOW-MEDIUM CONFIDENCE): TLS session renegotiation. If TLS session caching expired or was misconfigured, every external call now does a full TLS handshake instead of resuming. Three full handshakes could add 300-600ms total, contributing to but likely not fully explaining the 1600ms increase.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Check if TLS session reuse is enabled on the HTTP client. Look for increases in TLS handshake time in network traces."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 5 (LOW CONFIDENCE): Memory leak causing GC pauses. A slow memory leak could cause increasingly frequent garbage collection pauses, adding latency jitter that shows up at P99. The gradual onset fits this pattern.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Check memory usage trends over the two-week window. Look for sawtooth memory patterns. Monitor GC pause durations if using a managed runtime (JVM, .NET, Go)."},{"risk":"Sequential external calls amplify any single-service degradation","severity":"high","mitigation":"Parallelize independent external calls. If geocoding result is needed before carrier rates, at minimum parallelize geocoding with any other independent calls."},{"risk":"No circuit breaker means a degraded upstream can saturate the entire service","severity":"high","mitigation":"Add circuit breakers with latency-based tripping (not just error-based) to each external dependency. Set aggressive timeouts — if geocoding normally takes 50ms, a 500ms timeout with fallback is appropriate."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (IMMEDIATE — hours): Add per-external-call latency instrumentation. Log the start time, end time, and HTTP status for every outbound call to geocoding, carrier rates, and payment gateway. Deploy and collect data for 1 hour. This will definitively identify which service is the bottleneck. Phase 2 (SAME DAY): Once the slow service is identified, add a timeout (e.g., 2x the historical P99) and a circuit breaker. If geocoding is the culprit, add an LRU cache keyed on normalized address — geocoding results are immutable and highly cacheable. Phase 3 (THIS WEEK): Parallelize independent external calls. Add structured latency dashboards per dependency. Establish latency SLOs per external service so future degradation is caught within hours, not weeks.","files_to_create_or_modify":["middleware or interceptor for outbound HTTP calls (add timing instrumentation)","configuration for per-service timeouts and circuit breaker thresholds","caching layer for geocoding responses (if geocoding is confirmed as the culprit)","dashboard/alerting configuration for per-dependency P99 latency"],"patterns_to_follow":"Circuit Breaker (Hystrix/Resilience4j pattern), Bulkhead Isolation per external dependency, Cache-Aside for idempotent lookups (geocoding), Parallel Scatter-Gather for independent external calls, Structured Logging with correlation IDs for request tracing","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT add blanket caching without first identifying the slow service — you may cache the wrong thing","Do NOT set overly generous timeouts — a 30s timeout on a call that should take 50ms just delays the pain","Do NOT assume the DB dashboard tells the whole story — check if DB connection acquisition time (not query time) is also healthy","Do NOT add retries without timeouts — retrying a slow service without a timeout will make things worse","Do NOT confuse mean latency with P99 — the issue may only affect a subset of requests (e.g., cache misses on the upstream, specific address types that hit slow geocoding paths)"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Root cause is almost certainly in the external API call chain — the symptom profile (gradual, no errors, DB normal) is textbook upstream degradation","Per-dependency instrumentation is the correct immediate action regardless of which hypothesis is right","Sequential synchronous external calls are an architectural weakness that amplifies any upstream issue"],"low_confidence_areas":["Cannot determine WHICH specific external service is degraded without instrumentation data","Cannot rule out connection pool / DNS / TLS issues without network-level diagnostics","Unknown whether calls are already parallel or strictly sequential"],"assumptions":["Database dashboard accurately reflects actual DB latency including connection acquisition time","The three external APIs are called synchronously in the request path","No infrastructure changes (load balancer config, network routing, DNS resolver) occurred in the two-week window","The 10K requests/day volume has been roughly constant (ruling out simple load increase)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"A 9x increase in P99 latency (200ms → 1800ms) over two weeks, with no deployment correlation, normal DB metrics, and no errors, strongly points to degradation in one or more of the three external API dependencies — most likely the geocoding service or carrier rates API experiencing gradual throughput throttling or increased response times. Secondary hypotheses include connection pool exhaustion, DNS resolution degradation, or subtle resource leaks (memory/thread/connection)
